q: Fluid is a substance that offers no permanent resistance to ___.
The study of fluids has two parts, statics and ___.
Fluid statics concentrates on fluids at ___.
Fluid dynamics concerns with the fluid in ___.
Fluid statics includes atmospheric pressure, liquid pressure, Archimedes' principle and ___.
The study of the flow of liquid is called ___.
___ is the study of liquid at rest.
Surface tension and capillarity are important concepts of ___.
Students are expected to differentiate fluid flow as laminar and ___.
Students are expected to understand Bernoulli's principle as a relationship between pressure energy, kinetic energy and potential energy of a fluid in streamline (___) flow.
Students are expected to understand viscosity and ___' law.
Students are expected to distinguish between cohesion and adhesion and explain surface tension in ___.
Fluid is either liquid or ___.
The volume of a gas depends on pressure and temperature obeying Boyle's law and ___' law.
The volume of a liquid does not depend on pressure if the ___ is constant.
A liquid is incompressible, meaning its density is constant for any pressure provided that the temperature is kept ___.
In the flow of fluids, the path or flow of the fluid particles is called ___.
Motion of fluids can be classified by two types: laminar flow and ___ flow.
If fluid particles are moving steady in smooth paths in layers, with each layer moving smoothly past the adjacent layers with no mixing; such a flow is called ___ flow.
In steady laminar flow, streamlines do not ___ each other.
In steady laminar flow, every fluid particle arriving at a given point has the same ___.
If the flow or path of the fluid particles are irregular, their direction is always changing or whirling; this fluid movement is called ___ flow.
In turbulent flow, the speed of the fluid at a point is continuously undergoing changes in both magnitude and ___.
For ideal laminar fluid flow, the fluid is ___.
For ideal laminar fluid flow, the flow has no friction or friction is ___.
Internal friction in fluids is called ___.
For ideal laminar fluid flow, viscosity is considered to be ___.
For ideal laminar fluid flow, the fluid flow is ___ (steady).
___ flow is not taken into account for ideal laminar fluid flow.
Sea breeze is an example of ___ flow.
A storm is an example of ___ flow.
For an ideal fluid, there must be laminar flow and no ___.
The density of an ideal fluid is ___.
The mass flowing in section 1 must equal the mass flowing in ___.
Volume flow rate $\frac{V}{t}$ is the ___ throughout the pipe.
The equation $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ states that the liquid entering one end of the pipe leaves from the other end with a constant ___.
The equation $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ is known as the equation of ___ for fluid flow.
The larger the area of the pipe, the ___ is the speed.
The smaller the area of the pipe, the ___ is the speed.
The speed of water spraying from a hose increases by making the hose area ___.
Bernoulli's equation, in fluid dynamics, relates the pressure, flow speed, and height for the steady (laminar) flow of an ideal ___ fluid.
For an ideal incompressible fluid in Bernoulli's equation, the internal friction of the fluid flow is ___.
Bernoulli's equation was first derived in 1738 by the Swiss mathematician Daniel ___.
Bernoulli's equation can be derived using the ___ principle to a fluid flowing in a tube.
Bernoulli's theorem states that the sum of pressure energy, kinetic energy and potential energy per unit volume of an incompressible, non-viscous fluid in a streamlined laminar flow remains ___.
According to the work-energy principle, the work done on a section of fluid must equal the change in total ___ energy.
Total mechanical energy is the sum of kinetic energy and ___ energy.
Since the fluid is incompressible, $A_1 \Delta x_1 = A_2 \Delta x_2 = \text{___}$.
The work done on the fluid due to pressure is $W = (P_1 - \text{___})\Delta V$.
The change in kinetic energy is $\Delta KE = \frac{1}{2}\rho \Delta V (v_2^2 - \text{___})$.
The change in potential energy is $\Delta PE = \rho \Delta V g (h_2 - \text{___})$.
Bernoulli's equation is $p + \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 + \rho gh = \text{___}$.
The air flow moving above the top curved surface of the wing has a ___ distance to travel.
Air above the wing needs to go ___ to have the same transit time as air along the lower surface.
The velocity of air flow is high along the ___ surface of the wing.
The velocity of air flow is low along the ___ surface of the wing.
High velocity of air above the wing causes a ___ pressure.
Low velocity of air below the wing causes a ___ pressure.
The difference in pressure above and below the wing provides a ___ on the wing.
When the piston of the sprayer is pressed, the fast moving air causes a ___ pressure at the tip of the nozzle.
The liquid inside the sprayer container is at a ___ pressure.
The higher pressure inside the sprayer causes the liquid to rush up through the ___ and out the nozzle.
The Magnus effect is a particular manifestation of ___ theorem.
The Magnus effect is commonly associated with a ___ object moving through a fluid.
A spinning ball drags some of the ___ around with it.
The dragging of air by a spinning ball causes a difference in pressure of the air on opposite sides, producing the ___ of the ball.
The Magnus effect is named after the German physicist Heinrich Gustav ___.
Heinrich Gustav Magnus described the effect in ___.
During cyclones, moving air gains speed above the roof of a house, causing ___ pressure on the roof.
The pressure inside the house is ___ than the pressure above the roof during a cyclone.
The greater pressure inside the house during a cyclone can ___ the roof.
Most of the fluids are not ideal ones and offer some ___ to motion.
Resistance to fluid motion is like an internal friction analogous to friction when a ___ move on a surface.
The resistance to fluid motion (or) internal resistance of fluid is called ___.
Viscosity arises when there is ___ motion between layers of the fluid.
Viscosity measures resistance to flow arising due to the internal friction between the fluid layers as they slip past one ___.
Viscosity can also be thought of as a measure of a fluid's thickness (___).
A fluid with large viscosity resists motion because its strong intermolecular forces give it a lot of internal ___.
A fluid with low viscosity flows easily because its molecular make up results in very little friction when it is in ___.
Gases also exhibit viscosity, but it is harder to notice in ordinary ___.
The viscosity of liquids ___ rapidly with an increase in temperature.
The viscosity of gases ___ with an increase in temperature.
Upon heating, liquids flow ___ easily.
Upon heating, gases flow ___ slowly.
Viscosity does not change as the amount of matter changes, therefore it is an ___ property.
The SI unit of viscosity is ___ second (Pa s).
Pascal second (Pa s) is equivalent to ___.
c: newton second per metre squared (N s m$^{-2}$)

q: The SI unit of viscosity is sometimes referred to as the ___ (Pl).
There are two ways to measure fluid's viscosity: dynamic viscosity and ___ viscosity.
___ viscosity measures fluid's resistance to flow when an external force is applied.
___ viscosity measures the resistive flow of a fluid under the weight of gravity.
Newton's law of viscosity states that the ___ stress between the two adjacent layers of fluid is directly proportional to the negative value of the velocity gradient.
In symbols, shear stress $\tau \propto -\frac{dv}{dy}$ (or) $\tau = \text{___} \frac{dv}{dy}$.
Shear stress is the shearing force per unit ___.
Viscosity is defined as the ratio of shearing stress to the ___ gradient.
Stokes' law was derived by the British scientist Sir ___ in 1851.
Stokes' law expresses the drag force resisting the fall of small spherical objects through a fluid medium under the influence of ___.
The drag force $F$ acting upward in resistance to the fall of a spherical object in a viscous liquid is ___.
In Stokes' law, $\eta$ is the coefficient of viscosity of the liquid, $r$ is the radius of the sphere, and $v$ is the constant velocity of fall called the ___ velocity.
A simple way of measuring viscosity is that a small sphere ball is dropped through the fluid and time of fall and ___ of fall are measured.
The forces acting on a falling sphere ball are weight of the sphere ball, upward thrust, and ___ force.
Weight of the sphere ball is $\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_1 g$, where $\rho_1$ is the density of the ___.
Upward thrust is $\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_2 g$, where $\rho_2$ is the density of the ___.
The drag force on a spherical object in a viscous liquid is $F = \text{___}$.
At a constant velocity of fall (terminal velocity), the upward and downward forces are in ___.
Surface tension is the tendency of the surface of a liquid to behave like a stretched ___ skin (or membrane).
Surface tension is due to the attractive forces between liquid molecules called ___.
Cohesion refers to forces between molecules of the ___ substance.
Cohesion makes water molecules pull together making the water surface to stretch like an ___ skin.
Surface tension is the reason a ___ strider is able to stride along water surface without sinking.
Paperclips, razor blades and light coins are able to rest on water without sinking although their ___ is higher than that of water.
Surface tension is defined as the force per unit length that acts across any line on a surface tending to pull the surface ___.
Surface tension $\gamma = \frac{F}{l}$, where $F$ = force, $l$ = length, $\gamma$ = ___.
The SI unit of surface tension is ___ per metre (N m$^{-1}$).
Surface tension can also be referred to as a force because it is the tension between liquid ___.
Capillary action is defined as the spontaneous flow of a liquid into a ___ tube or porous material.
Capillary action is caused by the combination of cohesive forces of the liquid and the ___ forces between the liquid and tube material.
Adhesion refers to forces between molecules of ___ substances.
These cohesive and adhesive forces pull the liquid into the ___.
In order for wicking to occur, a tube needs to be sufficiently ___ in diameter.
Capillarity (or) capillary action is also the rise or depression of a liquid in a narrow tube of small ___.
The rise of water underneath the ground is an example of ___ action.
Capillary action helps bringing water up into the ___.
When a glass tube with a small inside diameter is dipped into water, the water ___ into the tube.
The smaller the bore of a capillary tube, the ___ the water will rise.
Liquids that rise in small bore tubes inserted into the liquid are said to ___ the tube.
Liquids that are depressed within thin tubes below the surface of the surrounding liquid do not ___ the tube.
Water is a liquid that wets glass capillary tubes; ___ is one that does not.
Capillarity does not occur for ___ liquids.
When a glass tube is dipped into water, the adhesion between glass and water causes a thin film of water to be drawn up over the inner and outer surfaces of the ___.
Since the ___ force is greater than cohesive force, water level rises until there is a sufficient mass of liquid for gravitational forces to overcome these intermolecular forces.
The actual amount of rise depends on the surface tension $\gamma$, the contact angle $\theta$ and the ___ $r$ of the tube.
The height $h$ to which the liquid can be lifted is given by $h=\frac{2\gamma \cos \theta}{\text{___} gr}$.
In the capillary rise formula, $\rho$ is the density of the ___.
